elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate how stellar feedback from the first stars (Population III) distributes metals through the interstellar and intergalactic medium using the star-by-star cosmological hydrodynamics simulation, \Aeos.
elements.sent-2411-002: We find that energy injected from the supernovae of the first stars is enough to expel a majority of gas and injected metals beyond the virial radius of halos with mass $M_*\lesssim10^7$ M$_\odot$, regardless of the number of supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-003: This prevents self-enrichment and results in a non-monotonic increase in metallicity at early times.
elements.sent-2411-004: Most minihalos ($M \gtrsim 10^5 \, \rm M_\odot$) do not retain significant fractions of the yields produced within their virial radii until they have grown to halo masses of $M \gtrsim 10^7 \, \rm M_\odot$.
elements.sent-2411-005: The loss of metals to regions well beyond the virial radius delays the onset of enriched star formation and extends the period that Population III star formation can persist.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also explore the contributions of different nucleosynthetic channels to 10 individual elements.
elements.sent-2411-007: On the timescale of the simulation (lowest redshift $z=14.3$), enrichment is dominated by core-collapse supernovae for all elements, but with a significant contribution from asymptotic giant branch winds to the s-process elements, which are normally thought to only be important at late times.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this work, we establish important mechanisms for early chemical enrichment which allows us to apply \Aeos in later epochs to trace the evolution of enrichment during the complete transition from Population III to Population II stars.
elements.sent-2411-009: At redshifts of $20 < z < 30$, mere hundreds of millions of years after the Big Bang, the first stars (Population III, or Pop III) began lighting up the Universe, ending the Dark Ages.
elements.sent-2411-010: Their contribution to the chemodynamical evolution of the Universe as well as their imprint on observed stars today are two of the major frontiers in astrophysics \citep{FrebelNorris2015,KlessenGlover2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In $\Lambda$-Cold Dark Matter cosmology, Pop III stars are expected to form from metal-free primordial gas that collapses in dark matter minihalos with masses of $10^{5}$--$10^{6}\rm{M _\odot$ \citep[e.g.][]{Haiman1996-1,Tegmark1997,Bromm1999,Abel2002,Yoshida2003,Kulkarni2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The lack of metals in primordial gas makes H$_2$ ro-vibrational lines the only viable process for cooling at low temperatures ($T<10^4$ K) \citep{Haiman1996-2,Tegmark1997 , resulting in cooling times longer than the free-fall time and, thus, large Jeans masses.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Pop III stars, then, are thought to have a top-heavy initial mass function.
elements.sent-2411-014: Many studies have suggested that Pop III stars were very massive, with masses $10^2$--$10^3 \,\rm M_\odot$ \citep{Abel2002,Bromm2002,Bromm2004,Yoshida2008,Hirano2015,Hosokawa2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, more recent work has demonstrated that fragmentation from turbulence \citep{Latif2013 , weak magnetic fields \citep{Sharda2020 , and gravitational instabilities in disks \citep{Stacy2010,Clark2011,Greif2011,Greif2012 , as well as suppression of accretion by UV radiation \citep{Hosokawa2011,Hosokawa2016,Hirano2014,Latif2022 can reduce the characteristic mass of Pop III stars to tens of solar masses or lower.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Regardless of the precise shape of the initial mass function, Pop III stars were likely too massive to survive until present day.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, their contribution to galactic and chemical evolution did not disappear upon their deaths through supernova (SN) explosions; instead they seeded their surrounding gas with the first metals from which the next generation of stars formed.
elements.sent-2411-018: Among them were slightly metal-enriched low-mass stars (Population II, or Pop II) \citep{Ritter2016,Chiaki2016,Chiaki2018,Chiaki2019,Magg2022,Chen2024 that survived to the present \citep{GnedinKravstov2006,Tumlinson2010,Griffen2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: These stars have retained the chemical fingerprints of Pop III in their atmospheres, which we can observe.
elements.sent-2411-020: Searching for these rare metal-poor stars is a non-trivial pursuit, but many have been found in the stellar halo as well as across nearly all dwarf satellite galaxies including the ultra-faint dwarf galaxies \citep[UFDs;][]{FrebelNorris2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: UFDs inefficiently formed stars until being quenched by reionization \citep{Brown2014 and are the ideal cosmic labs in which to study these stars.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, UFDs serve as foundational building blocks of larger galaxies, and our own Milky Way's stellar halo is likely comprised of their remnants.
elements.sent-2411-023: The chemical abundances of metal-poor stars in the UFDs and the stellar halo are promising ways to investigate early galaxy formation and chemical enrichment \citep[e.g.,][]{Ji2015,Brauer19 , and stellar chemical abundances have been studied through high-resolution spectroscopy and wide-field spectroscopic surveys such as APOGEE \citep{apogee_overview , GALAH \citep{GALAH , LAMOST \citep{LAMOST,LAMOST_spec , and Gaia RVS \citep{GaiaMission,GaiaDR3 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: A few stars have been found whose abundances suggest they have been enriched by a single progenitor SN \citep[][]{Ezzeddine2019, Skuladottir2021,Yong2021,Xing2023,Skuladottir2024,Ji2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, our understanding of the progenitors of these stars rest heavily on our assumptions about the explosion mechanisms and yields of Pop III SNe \citep[e.g.][]{HegerWoosley2002,HegerWoosley2010 and the dilution and mixing of metals in the interstellar and intergalactic medium (ISM and IGM).}
elements.sent-2411-026: Thus, having a theoretical understanding of the chemical evolution of the first galaxies, ignited by the first generations of stars, has become critical to moving forward with interpreting the history of our Galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-027: Cosmological hydrodynamics simulations aimed at studying the chemodynamical evolution of galaxies have been largely successful at reproducing galaxy-scale relations such as the mass-metallicity relation \citep{Obreja2014,Ma2016,Dave2017,DeRossi2017,Hirai2017-2,Torrey2019,Agertz2020,Marszewski2024 , metallicity distribution functions, and the evolution of individual chemical abundances \citep{Marcolini2008,Revaz2009,Sawala2010,RevazJablonka2012,Jeon2017,Hirai2017-1,Hirai2017-2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Cosmological simulations cannot directly model small-scale physical processes given the large dynamic range, instead relying on sub-grid models to include these phenomena \citep[see][and references therein]{Crain2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Among these is the mixing of chemically inhomogeneous particles and regions.
elements.sent-2411-030: Until recently, the assumption of homogeneous mixing was sufficient to reproduce observed global abundance trends across galaxies, but the influx of copious amounts of chemical abundance data for Milky Way and Local Group stars in the last decade has unveiled new variations in the chemical abundances of coeval and co-natal populations of stars \citep{Hill2019,Ji2020,Mead2024 that can only be explained by more complex evolutionary processes.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Recent computational advances have opened the door to more directly modeling smaller-scale physics in cosmological simulations by increasing both mass and spatial resolution, thereby reducing the dependence on sub-grid models.
elements.sent-2411-032: This has been a critical and necessary advance toward understanding the more complex galaxy formation processes that drive chemical evolution, particularly in the early universe and dwarf galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-033: These higher resolution simulations have begun to model single stars as individual particles \citep{Emerick2019,Gutcke+2021,Lahen2020,Lahen+2023,Andersson2023,Andersson2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: This enables the study of individual stellar feedback and how the details of when and where individual stars inject energy and enriched material impact the chemodynamical evolution of their environment.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this paper, we use \Aeos, a new star-by-star cosmological hydrodynamics simulation \citep[][hereafter, \citetalias{AeosMethods ]{AeosMethods , to study the chemical enrichment of minihalos following Pop III and the first Pop II SNe, and the transport of metals between halos and the IGM.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Theory and simulations have demonstrated that dwarf galaxies can efficiently enrich their circumgalactic medium (CGM) and the IGM \citep{DekelSilk1986,MacLowFerrara1999,Fragile2004,Muratov2017 , showing that outflows of enriched material are primarily dependent on the mass of the halo and the location of the SNe.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Large halos and halos with spatially dispersed SNe are more likely to retain their metals.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, studies of these outflows of enriched material have thus far been limited to halos $M_{\rm vir > 10^6 \, \rm M_\odot$ or low redshift ($z<4$).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, studies on the distribution of metals in and beyond host galaxies have been extended to individual elements from different nucleosynthetic origins in theoretical models \citep{KrumholzTing2018 and in hydrodynamic simulations \citep{Emerick2018b .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: These works have found that the mixing and retention of individual elements in a galaxy are dependent on their astrophysical origin.
elements.sent-2411-041: With \Aeos, we are now able to extend these studies to high redshift minihalos that are the progenitors to dwarf galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-042: This paper is organized as follows: in Section \ref{sec:sims we describe the \Aeos model, but we refer the reader to \citetalias{AeosMethods for additional details.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We explore the behavior of metals in the multiphase ISM and IGM in Section \ref{sec:met_overview and characterize the loss of metals from halos to the IGM in Section \ref{sec:met_loss .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: In Section \ref{sec:indiv_elem , we take a look at the individual elements traced in \Aeos, specifically the contributions from different nucleosynthetic sources, and their individual behaviors moving from the ISM to IGM.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:conclusions , we summarize our findings and discuss the implications of our results for future work in numerical and analytical models.}

elements.sent-2411-046: The methods used in these simulations are described in \citetalias{AeosMethods , and for the case of an idealized galaxy, in \citet[][hereafter, \citetalias{Emerick2019 ]{Emerick2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The description here is limited to a summary of the properties and physics relevant to this paper.
elements.sent-2411-048: We follow non-equilibrium primordial chemistry using Grackle \citep{GrackleMethod with radiative heating and self-shielding from a \citet{HM2012 UV background.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: To limit the number of radiation sources while still capturing the vast majority of the photon energy budget, we restrict radiation to massive stars with $M_* >$8~M$_{\odot}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: Ionizing radiation from massive stars is tracked using the {\sc Moray adaptive ray-tracing radiative transfer method \citep{WiseAbel2011 .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Additionally, we trace non-ionizing UV radiation from massive stars that causes photoelectric heating and Lyman-Werner (LW) dissociation of H$_2$.
elements.sent-2411-052: We track stellar feedback from supernovae and stellar winds, and follow yields for 10 metal species (C, N, O, Na, Mg, Ca, Mn, Fe, Sr, Ba) in addition to the total metal density.
elements.sent-2411-053: At this resolution, the Jeans length can become unresolved, leading to artificial fragmentation.
elements.sent-2411-054: While in \citetalias{Emerick2019 , a pressure floor was used to support gas against artificial fragmentation, \Aeos does not use one, instead setting the star formation density sufficiently low to avoid under-resolving the Jeans length and relying on the efficient conversion of dense gas into stars.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Stars form stochastically if the following conditions are met by assuming that the local star formation rate is proportional to an efficiency per free-fall time, which we take as $\epsilon_{\rm ff = 0.02$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The conditions for star formation are: i) $\nabla \cdot v < 0$ (converging gas flow), ii) $T < 500$ K, and iii) $n > 10^4$ cm$^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-057: In the simulation, both Pop III and Pop II star formation are treated star-by-star, meaning that each star particle that produces feedback on the timescale of the simulation represents an individual star with a mass sampled from the adopted IMF.
elements.sent-2411-058: In addition to the constraints above, Pop III star formation in our simulation also requires a minimum molecular hydrogen fraction ($f_{H_2 > 0.0005$) that is consistent with high-resolution simulations of Pop III star formation at $n > 10^4$ cm$^{-3}$ \citep{Susa2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: To distinguish Pop III from Pop II stars, we use a metallicity limit $Z < 10^{-5 \rm Z_\odot$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Pop III stellar masses are sampled from a Salpeter IMF \citep{Salpeter1955 with a power-law slope $\alpha = 2.3$ for masses above characteristic mass, $M_{\rm char}$, and an exponential cut-off below \citep[following][]{Wise2012a .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: As mentioned in \citetalias{AeosMethods , there are two runs of the simulation which use different mass ranges and characteristic masses for Pop III.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In this paper, we use the A{\sc eos20 simulation which has a minimum Pop III mass of 1 $\rm M_\odot$ and maximum mass of 300 $\rm M_\odot$, with $M_{\rm char}$ = 20 $\rm M_\odot$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Thus, most of our Pop III stars will have masses between $20-300 \rm M_\odot$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Pop III stars are assigned lifetimes from \citet{Schaerer2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Pop II star formation occurs when the metallicity threshold described for Pop III stars is exceeded, and does not require an H$_2$ threshold.
elements.sent-2411-066: Pop II stellar masses are sampled from a Kroupa IMF \citep{Kroupa2001 with a minimum mass of 0.08 $\rm M_\odot$ and a maximum mass of 120 $\rm M_\odot$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Stars above 2 $\rm M_\odot$ are treated as individual star particles, however, stars below that threshold in a star forming cell are grouped together into a single particle as they do not contribute to feedback or metal enrichment on the timescales of this simulation.
elements.sent-2411-068: We set stellar radii, effective temperature, surface gravity, lifetime, and AGB phase using the stellar evolution data from PARSEC \citep{Bressan2012 .}

elements.sent-2411-069: We implement multi-channel stellar feedback, including core-collapse supernovae (CCSNe) for both Pop III and Pop II stars, Type Ia supernovae (SNIa), and stellar winds for Pop II stars.
elements.sent-2411-070: Each feedback channel is discussed below.
elements.sent-2411-071: Stellar Winds: There are two types of stellar winds: asymptotic giant branch (AGB) stellar winds and winds from massive stars.
elements.sent-2411-072: Winds are deposited onto the grid over a 2 pc radius.
elements.sent-2411-073: As resolving the fast hot winds is computationally expensive (see \citetalias{Emerick2019 ), the wind velocity for all massive stars is fixed over their lifetimes at a maximum value of 100 km s$^{-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: The kinetic energy of the winds is fully thermalized before injection.
elements.sent-2411-075: Mass loss rates are assumed to be constant for all winds over the lifetime of the star.
elements.sent-2411-076: Supernovae: We implement two different types of supernovae: CCSNe and SNIa.
elements.sent-2411-077: With a maximum resolution of 1 proper pc in these simulations, we are able to resolve the Sedov-Taylor phase of the vast majority of our supernovae at the typical gas densities in which they explode \citep{Smith2018b,Hu2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Therefore, for these events, we only include the thermal energy deposition.
elements.sent-2411-079: Energy and mass from each supernova explosion are deposited over a 2 pc radius sphere.
elements.sent-2411-080: Core-Collapse Supernovae: Pop III stars of masses $10 \, \rm M_\odot$ $< M_* \, <$ $100 \, \rm M_\odot$ and Pop II stars of masses $8 \, \rm M_\odot$ $< \, M_* \, <$ $25 \, \rm M_\odot$ explode as CCSNe with a fixed energy of $10^{51}$~erg.
elements.sent-2411-081: Stars above these ranges undergo direct collapse to a black hole at the end of their lifetimes with no mass or energy feedback.
elements.sent-2411-082: Though pair-instability supernovae are expected to occur for stars in the range of $140-260 \rm M_\odot$, we do not include these in our current simulations.
elements.sent-2411-083: Type Ia Supernovae: Pop II stars $M_* < 8\, \rm M_\odot$ become white dwarfs, with stars $3 \, {\rm M_\odot < M_* < 8 \,\rm M_\odot$ being capable of exploding as SNIa.}
elements.sent-2411-084: For SNIa, we use the "standard" delay time distribution model in \citet{Ruiter2011 , which sums four different channels: 1) double degenerate, 2) single degenerate, 3) helium-rich donor, and 4) a sub-Chandrasekhar mass.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We use a random number draw from this distribution to determine when and if a white dwarf will explode as a SNIa.
elements.sent-2411-086: SNIa explode with a fixed energy of $10^{51}$~erg.
elements.sent-2411-087: We capture detailed chemical yields for Pop III and Pop II CCSNe, SNIa, and stellar winds including C, N, O, Na, Mg, Ca, Mn, Fe, Sr, and Ba metal abundances in addition to H, He, and overall metallicity, Z.
elements.sent-2411-088: The individual metal abundances trace different channels of feedback and progenitor mass.
elements.sent-2411-089: O, Mg, and Ca primarily trace CCSNe, and trace short timescales of chemical evolution (10 Myr) \citep{Timmes1995,Kobayashi2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The evolution between SNIa and CCSNe is traced by the relative abundances of O, Mg, and Ca to Fe, which tracks chemical evolution on timescales of 100 Myr to 1 Gyr \citep{Tinsley1980,Matteucci1986,Hayden2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: S-process enrichment from low-mass AGB stars is traced using N, Ba (4-8 $\rm M_\odot$), and Sr (\textless 4 $\rm M_\odot$) \citep[e.g.][and references therein]{Karakas2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Pop III CCSNe Yields: Pop III CCSNe ($10 \, \rm M_\odot$ $< \, M_* \, <$ $100 \, \rm M_\odot$) yields are adopted from \citet{HegerWoosley2010 using standard 0.1 mixing.}
elements.sent-2411-093: It is reasonable to approximate that all stars up to 100 $\rm M_\odot$ end their lives as CCSNe, though stars in the range 70-120 $\rm M_\odot$ may end their lives as either CCSNe or by directly collapsing to a black hole \citep{Woosley2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-094: Pop II CCSNe Yields: Yields for Pop II CCSNe were adopted from \citet{Limongi2018 using 9 grid points in mass ($13 \, \rm M_\odot$ $\leq \, M_* \, \leq$ $120 \, \rm M_\odot$) and 4 grid points in metallicity ($3.236\times10^{-5 \leq Z_* \leq 0.01345$).}}
elements.sent-2411-095: We use linear interpolation between grid points on the table.
elements.sent-2411-096: Stars with masses outside of the grid points adopt the abundance ratios of the nearest grid point and scale the yields linearly with mass.
elements.sent-2411-097: Stars with metallicities outside of the grid points adopt the yields of the closest grid point.
